does: This paper introduces ASRU, a framework for unlearning sensitive cross-modal information from multimodal large language models while maintaining generation quality. The method operates in two stages: first, activation steering redirects internal model representations to induce refusal behavior for target knowledge, and second, reinforcement learning with a customized reward function fine-tunes refusal boundaries to optimize the trade-off between forgetting and utility preservation. Evaluated on Qwen3-VL, ASRU achieves 24.6% improvement in unlearning effectiveness and 5.8X improvement in generation quality compared to baseline approaches, using only small amounts of retained supervision data for training.
connects: This work extends the emerging field of machine unlearning, which has primarily focused on vision and NLP models, toward the more complex multimodal setting where models must forget sensitive cross-modal associations. It builds on activation engineering and steering techniques from recent interpretability work that manipulates internal representations to control model behavior, while incorporating reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) methods now standard in language model alignment. The paper responds to a critical limitation in prior unlearning approaches—their focus on output deviation metrics without considering the usability degradation, hallucination, and semantic drift that occur when models are constrained without guidance, a problem identified but underexplored in recent machine unlearning literature.
enables: This work opens directions for safer deployment of multimodal models by demonstrating that unlearning need not sacrifice model utility or coherence. It suggests activation steering combined with RL-based objectives could be applied to other problematic knowledge types in multimodal settings, such as biases or harmful associations between text and images. The framework's emphasis on generation quality as a core unlearning objective could influence future safety evaluation standards beyond simple forgetting metrics. Additionally, the approach may enable more practical consent-based and privacy-respecting model deployment scenarios where organizations can remove specific sensitive training data influences while maintaining general capabilities, and could inform techniques for controlling hallucinations or other quality issues in multimodal systems.
